blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلنَّـٰزِعَـٰتِ غَرْقًا (١)
By those [angels] who wrest [the soul] violently, (1)
وَٱلنَّـٰشِطَـٰتِ نَشْطًا (٢)
by those who draw [it] out gently, (2)
وَٱلسَّـٰبِحَـٰتِ سَبْحًا (٣)
by those who swim smoothly, (3)
فَٱلسَّـٰبِقَـٰتِ سَبْقًا (٤)
by those who, racing, take the lead, (4)
فَٱلْمُدَبِّرَٰتِ أَمْرًا (٥)
by those who direct the affairs [of creatures]: (5)

Contents of Surah "An-Nazi'at"

This Surah, like Surah "An-Naba," revolves around the issues of the "Resurrection" and can be summarized into six sections:

  1. First, it emphasizes and insists upon the realization of this Great Day through emphatic oaths related to the matter of the Resurrection.

  2. In the next stage, it points to some of the terrifying and horrific scenes of that Day.

  3. In another section, it provides a brief and passing reference to the story of Moses and the fate of the rebellious Pharaoh, which serves both as a consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers, and as a warning to the rebellious polytheists; it also indicates what sins the denial of the Resurrection can lead a person into.

  4. In the following section, it enumerates examples of the manifestations of Allah's power in the heavens and the earth, which themselves serve as proof for the possibility of the Resurrection and life after death.

  5. Once again, it proceeds to describe another part of the terrifying events of that Great Day, the fate of the transgressors, and the reward of the righteous.

  6. Finally, at the end of the Surah, it emphasizes the fact that no one knows the timing of that Day, but it is certain that it is near.

The name "An-Nazi'at" was chosen for this Surah because of its first verse.

The Virtue of Reciting this Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it is stated:

من قرأ سورة "و النازعات" لم يكن حبسه و حسابه يوم القيامة الا كقدر صلاة مكتوبة حتى يدخل الجنة:

"Whoever recites Surah An-Nazi'at, his detention and accounting on the Day of Resurrection will only last as long as the duration of a daily obligatory prayer, until he enters Paradise." [1] Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) says:

من قرأها لم يمت الا ريان، و لم يبعثه اللَّه الا ريان، و لم يدخله الجنة الا ريان:

"Whoever recites it will not die except quenched (of thirst), and Allah will not resurrect him except quenched, and He will not enter him into Paradise except quenched" (quenched by the infinite mercy of the Truth). [2] It is certain that whoever implements the content of this Surah—whose soul-shaking verses awaken sleeping spirits and make them mindful of their duties—within their own soul, will enjoy such rewards; not those who merely content themselves with reciting the words.

[1] and [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 428.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 26, p. 73

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ وَ النَّازِعاتِ غَرْقاً (1) وَ النَّاشِطاتِ نَشْطاً (2) وَ السَّابِحاتِ سَبْحاً (3) فَالسَّابِقاتِ سَبْقاً (4) فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً (5)

Commentary

An Oath by These Diligent Angels

In these verses, an oath is taken by five important subjects, and the purpose of these oaths is to state the truth and the realization of the matter of the Resurrection. He says:

"By those [angels] who pull out [the souls of the wicked] with violence," (وَ النَّازِعاتِ غَرْقاً)

"And by those who draw out [the souls of the blessed] with gentleness," (وَ النَّاشِطاتِ نَشْطاً)

"And by those who glide [as if] swimming [swiftly]," (وَ السَّابِحاتِ سَبْحاً)

"And those who press forward as in a race," (فَالسَّابِقاتِ سَبْقاً)

"And those who regulate the affair [by Allah's command]," (فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً) Before we proceed to the commentary of these verses, the vocabulary used in them must be precisely clarified.

"Nazi'at" is from the root "naz'" (نزع), meaning to uproot something or to pull it, such as pulling a bow when shooting an arrow. Sometimes this word is also used for spiritual matters, such as "naz'" of enmity or love from the heart, meaning to uproot it. [1] "Gharq" (غرق) with a fatha on the ra (rhyming with shafaq), according to many lexicographers, originally means sinking in water, and sometimes it has come to mean being severely caught in an accident or calamity.

And "gharq" (rhyming with farq), according to Ibn Manzur in Lisan al-Arab, is a noun that replaces the infinitive (masdar) and means "ighraq" (إغراق). Ighraq originally means drawing a bow to the furthest possible point; subsequently, it came to mean exaggeration or going to the limit in any action.

From this, it becomes clear that what appears in the above verse does not mean "drowning," but rather performing a task to the utmost possible extent. [1] "Nashitat" is from the root "nasht" (نشط), which originally means untying knots that are easily opened. Wells that are shallow and from which the bucket comes out easily with a single movement are called "inshat," and camels that move quickly with a slight hint are called "nashitah." Therefore, generally, this word is used for any kind of movement performed with ease.

"Sabihat" is from the root "sabh" (سبح), meaning rapid movement in water or air. Thus, it is used for swimming in water, the swift movement of a horse, or going after a task with speed. "Tasbih," which means declaring Allah free from any defect or deficiency, is also derived from this meaning, as if the one who performs tasbih moves forward with speed in the worship of the Lord.

"Sabiqat" is from the root "sabqat," meaning to precede or overtake. Since overtaking is usually impossible without speed, the concept of speed is sometimes inferred from this root.

"Mudabbirat" is from the root "tadbir," meaning to consider the end of something. Since considering the end and foresight causes a task to be organized in the best possible way, the word "tadbir" is also used in this sense.

Now, considering what has been said regarding the meaning of the words in these verses, we turn to their commentary.

What persons or things do these five oaths—which are initially shrouded in a veil of ambiguity, an ambiguity that motivates deeper and more profound "thought" and causes the mind and intellect to wander, scrutinize, and investigate—refer to?

Commentators have spoken much about this and have many interpretations, which mainly revolve around three axes:

  1. These oaths refer to the "angels" who are commissioned to seize the souls of the disbelievers and criminals, pulling them out of their bodies with severity—souls that were never willing to submit to the Truth. And the angels who are commissioned to seize the souls of the believers, separating them with leniency, gentleness, and ease. And the angels who move with speed in executing the Divine command. Then they overtake one another. And finally, they regulate the affairs of the world by His command.

  2. These oaths refer to the "stars" of the heaven, which are constantly being uprooted from one horizon and heading toward another. A group moves calmly, and a group travels with speed. And they are swimming in the ocean of the upper world. And they overtake one another. And finally, through the effects these celestial bodies have (effects such as the influence of sunlight and moonlight on the globe), they regulate the affairs of the world by Allah's command.

  3. They refer to the warriors or the horses of the mujahideen in the way of Allah, who are pulled away from their homes and homelands with intensity. Then they take the path to the battlefield with vigor and ease. And they overtake one another. And they regulate and manage the affairs of war.

Sometimes some commentators have mixed these interpretations, taking part of the five oaths from one interpretation and another part from another; however, the principles are the three interpretations mentioned above. [1] Of course, there is no contradiction between these interpretations, and it is possible that the above verses refer to all of them. However, overall, the first interpretation is the most appropriate, considering the points mentioned below:

First is the compatibility with the Day of Resurrection, which the Surah as a whole oversees. Another is the compatibility with similar verses at the beginning of Surah "Al-Mursalat."

[1] Some have suggested a fourth possibility: that these oaths refer to the "natural," "voluntary," and "industrial" movements of the beings of the world. For example, a sperm in its natural movement is first uprooted from its original place, the father's loins; then, once placed in the mother's womb, it continues its path gently; then it gains speed, and the vital materials of the sperm overtake one another, and finally, they form a complete human and regulate him. In voluntary movements, a human first makes a decision, then starts moving slowly, then gains speed, then tries to overtake others in his path, and finally proceeds to regulate his own affairs and social life. Similarly, industrial means like airplanes go through these stages in their movement (but this interpretation lacks clear evidence).

Furthermore, the phrase "and those who regulate the affair" (فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً) has the greatest compatibility with the angels who regulate the affairs of the world by Allah's command and do not hesitate for a moment in carrying out His orders: "They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command" (Al-Anbiya: 27). Especially since the matter of regulating affairs is mentioned here in an absolute manner without any restriction or condition.

Beyond all this, narrations have been quoted from the Infallible Imams in the commentary of these verses that are consistent with this very meaning, including:

In a narration from Ali (عليه السلام), we read that regarding the commentary of "By those who pull out with violence," he said: "It refers to the angels who pull the souls of the disbelievers out of their bodies with severity, just as an archer draws a bow to the final stage." [1] A similar meaning has been narrated from him (عليه السلام) regarding the commentary of "And by those who draw out," as well as the commentary of "And by those who glide" and "And those who regulate." [2] Of course, this interpretation can be stated in a more general and universal way, such that the matter of seizing the souls of the believers and disbelievers is one of its instances, not its entire content. In this way, it could be said that these oaths collectively refer to the angels in relation to the execution of all Divine commands, which are realized in five stages: first, the initial intense movement following a decisive resolution; then the stage of moving calmly; then gaining speed; then overtaking; and finally, regulating the affairs and organizing the tasks. In any case, the program of the angels in the field of seizing the souls of the disbelievers and believers is one of the instances of this general concept and serves as a precursor to the future discussions of this Surah regarding the Resurrection.

Answer to Two Questions

Two questions remain here: first, "Nazi'at" and "Nashitat" are feminine plurals; for what reason are they used here? Second, the first three oaths begin with the "waw" of swearing, but in the fourth and fifth oaths, "fa" (ف) has replaced it, which is for conjunction or sequence.

In response to the first question, one must note that "Nazi'at" is the plural of "Nazi'ah," meaning a party or group of angels that performs this program; likewise "Nashitat" and the rest of these plural forms. Since "party" (ta'ifah) is grammatically feminine, its plural is formed in this manner.

In response to the second question, it can be said that overtaking is the result of the rapid movement inferred from "Sabihat," and the regulation of affairs is a result of the totality of this movement.

The final word to be said here is that all these oaths are for the purpose of stating a matter that is not explicitly mentioned in the verse, but is clearly understood from the context and also from the subsequent verses. The goal is to say: "By these matters, all of you will eventually be resurrected and gathered, and the Resurrection and the Rising are the Truth." [1]

[1] And the implied meaning is: "You shall certainly be resurrected on the Day of Judgment, and you shall certainly be gathered and held to account."

Ayahs

يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ ٱلرَّاجِفَةُ (٦)
the day when the Quaker quakes (6)
تَتْبَعُهَا ٱلرَّادِفَةُ (٧)
and is followed by the Successor, (7)
قُلُوبٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاجِفَةٌ (٨)
hearts will be trembling on that day, (8)
أَبْصَـٰرُهَا خَـٰشِعَةٌ (٩)
bearing a humbled look. (9)
يَقُولُونَ أَءِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِى ٱلْحَافِرَةِ (١٠)
They will say, ‘Are we being returned to our earlier state? (10)
أَءِذَا كُنَّا عِظَـٰمًا نَّخِرَةً (١١)
What, even after we have been decayed bones?!’ (11)
قَالُوا۟ تِلْكَ إِذًا كَرَّةٌ خَاسِرَةٌ (١٢)
They will say, ‘This is, then, a ruinous return!’ (12)
فَإِنَّمَا هِىَ زَجْرَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ (١٣)
Yet it will be only a single shout, (13)
فَإِذَا هُم بِٱلسَّاهِرَةِ (١٤)
and behold, they will be awake. (14)

Commentary

Resurrection Occurs with Only a Single Mighty Blast!

After the occurrence of the Resurrection was established as an inevitable matter through emphatic## Commentary

Resurrection Occurs with Only a Single Mighty Blast!

After the occurrence of the Resurrection was established as an inevitable matter through emphatic oaths in the preceding verses, the verses under discussion proceed to describe some of the signs and events of this Great Day, stating: "This resurrection and rising will occur on the day when the terrifying earthquake makes everything tremble" (يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الرَّاجِفَةُ).

"Then the second great event follows it" (تَتْبَعُهَا الرَّادِفَةُ). "Rājifah" is derived from the root "rajf" (on the pattern of kashf), meaning agitation and intense shaking. Since provocative news causes agitation in society, it is referred to as "arājīf."

"Rādifah" is derived from the root "ridf" (on the pattern of harf), meaning a person or thing that follows another; hence, a person who rides behind another on a mount is called a radīf.

Many commentators believe that "Rājifah" refers to the first cry or the first blowing of the Trumpet, which is the trumpet of the world's annihilation and the earthquake of the destruction of the universe. "Rādifah" refers to the second cry or the second blowing of the Trumpet, which is the blast of life, resurrection, and the return to a new existence [1]. Therefore, the verse is similar to what appears in verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar: وَ نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرى‌ فَإِذا هُمْ قِيامٌ يَنْظُرُونَ: "And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will fall dead except whom Allah wills. Then it will be blown again, and at once they will be standing, looking on."

Some have also said that "Rājifah" refers to the earthquake that disintegrates the earth, and "Rādifah" is the earthquake that shatters the heavens, but the first interpretation seems more correct.

Then He adds: "Hearts, that Day, will be in extreme agitation" (قُلُوبٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ واجِفَةٌ). The hearts of the criminals, sinners, and transgressors will all tremble violently, anxious about the reckoning, the recompense, and the punishment.

"Wājifah" is from the root "wajf" (on the pattern of hadhf), which originally means speed of travel. Awjaftu al-ba‘īr is said when a person makes a camel move with speed, and since rapid movement causes shaking and agitation, this word is also used to mean "intense agitation."

This internal agitation is so severe that its effects appear in the entire being of the sinners; thus, in the next verse He adds: "Their eyes will be downcast and humbled from the intensity of fear" (أَبْصارُها خاشِعَةٌ) [1]. On that day, the eyes will sink into their sockets, stop moving, and stare blankly, as if they lose their sight from the intensity of fear.

Then the discourse shifts from the Resurrection to this world, stating: "Despite all this, they say in this world: 'Shall we indeed be returned to our former state?'" (يَقُولُونَ أَ إِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِي الْحافِرَةِ). "Hāfirah" is from the root "hafr," which originally means digging the ground, and the mark left by it is called a "hufrah" (hole). A horse's hoof is called "hāfir" because it digs the ground. Subsequently, "hāfirah" was used as a metaphor for the original state, because a person digs the ground with his feet on the path he travels, leaving footprints, and when he returns, he steps into those same "original holes." Therefore, this word has come to mean the "original state" [2].

The next verse continues their speech, saying: "What! When we have become decayed and scattered bones, shall we be returned to life again?!" (أَ إِذا كُنَّا عِظاماً نَخِرَةً) [1]. This is the very point that the deniers of the Resurrection always relied upon, saying: It is unbelievable that decayed bone should once again be clothed in the garment of life, for they saw the distance between it and a living being as vast, while they had forgotten that they were created from that same dust in the beginning. "Nakhirah" is an adjective derived from the root "nakhr" (on the pattern of nakhl and also shajar), originally meaning a decayed and hollow tree that makes a sound when the wind blows through it. Thus, the sound that resonates in the nose is called "nakhīr." Subsequently, this word has been used for any decayed and disintegrated entity.

The deniers of the Resurrection do not stop at this; rather, they rise to mock the Resurrection and say derisively: "If there is to be a Resurrection, it would then be a return of loss, and our situation on that day will be difficult and painful!" (قالُوا تِلْكَ إِذاً كَرَّةٌ خاسِرَةٌ). Another possibility in the interpretation of this verse is that they say this seriously and not mockingly, meaning that if there is a return, it is a repetitive and futile return, or rather a harmful one. If life is good, it would be better for Allah to continue this one, and if it is bad, what is the purpose of returning? The phrase "أَ إِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِي الْحافِرَةِ," considering that "hāfirah" means a pit or hole, could also be a clue for this interpretation, but the most famous among the commentators is the first interpretation.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verses, the expression "yaqūlūn" (they say) was used, which indicates the continuity and repetition of that speech by them. However, the expression "qālū" (they said) in the verse under discussion is evidence that they did not repeat this particular statement always; it only escaped them occasionally. This is the subtle point of difference in these expressions.

At the end of these verses, He returns once more to the issue of the rising of the Resurrection and the establishment of the Day of Judgment, stating in a decisive and crushing tone: "This return will occur with only a single cry and a mighty shout" (فَإِنَّما هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ). "Suddenly they will all stand up and appear upon the surface of the earth" (فَإِذا هُمْ بِالسَّاهِرَةِ).

This is not a complex or difficult task; as soon as the second blast is blown by Allah's command and the call of life and existence arises, all these dusts and decayed bones will suddenly gather, take soul, and spring forth from the graves! "Zajrah" originally means a shout used for driving away, and here it means the second blast. The choice of this expression (زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ), considering the content of these two words, points to the speed and suddenness of the Resurrection and its ease and simplicity before the power of Allah. With one commanding shout from the Angel of Resurrection, or the Trumpet of Israfil, all the dead will clothe themselves in the garment of life and be present in the arena of gathering for the reckoning.

"Sāhirah" is from the root "sahar" (on the pattern of sahar), meaning wakefulness at night. Since terrifying events take away sleep from the eyes at night, and the land of Resurrection is also very horrifying, the word "sāhirah" has been applied to the arena of gathering. Some have also said that "sāhirah" is applied to any desert, because fundamentally all deserts are frightening, as if they take away sleep from the eyes due to terror [1].

[^1]: It should be noted that the root "rajf" has appeared both as a transitive and an intransitive verb. In the first case, "Rājifah" means that great earthquake that makes the earth and all beings tremble, and in the second case, "Rājifah" means the earth itself which is shaken (take note).

[^1]: The pronoun in "absāruhā" (their eyes) refers to "qulūb" (hearts), which here means "souls and spirits." This attribution is because the impressions of the human senses all stem from the spirit and soul of man, and the source of the agitation and terror seen in the eyes is the terror that has cast its shadow over the soul.

[^2]: It should be noted that the active participle here is in the sense of the passive participle, and "hāfirah" means "mahfūrah" (that which is dug).

[^1]: This sentence has an omitted part, and its estimation is: "When we have become decayed bones, shall we be returned alive?" or "Shall we indeed be resurrected?"

[^1]: Lisān al-‘Arab, root s-h-r; Majma‘ al-Bayān, vol. 10, p. 429; Tafsīr al-Qurtubī, vol. 10, p. 6990.

Ayahs

هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰٓ (١٥)
Did you receive the story of Moses, (15)
إِذْ نَادَىٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ بِٱلْوَادِ ٱلْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى (١٦)
when his Lord called out to him in the holy valley of Tuwa? (16)
ٱذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ (١٧)
[And said,] ‘Go to Pharaoh, for indeed he has rebelled, (17)
فَقُلْ هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰٓ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ (١٨)
and say, ‘‘Would you purify yourself? (18)
وَأَهْدِيَكَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَتَخْشَىٰ (١٩)
I will guide you to your Lord, that you may fear [Him]?’’ ’ (19)
فَأَرَىٰهُ ٱلْـَٔايَةَ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰ (٢٠)
Then he showed him the greatest sign. (20)
فَكَذَّبَ وَعَصَىٰ (٢١)
But he denied, and disobeyed. (21)
ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ يَسْعَىٰ (٢٢)
Then he turned back, walking swiftly, (22)
فَحَشَرَ فَنَادَىٰ (٢٣)
and gathered [the people] and proclaimed, (23)
فَقَالَ أَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (٢٤)
saying, ‘I am your exalted lord!’ (24)
فَأَخَذَهُ ٱللَّهُ نَكَالَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَٱلْأُولَىٰٓ (٢٥)
So Allah seized him with the punishment of this life and the Hereafter. (25)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰٓ (٢٦)
There is indeed a moral in that for someone who fears! (26)

Commentary

Pharaoh Claimed, "I Am Your Great Lord!"

Following the relatively detailed explanations in the preceding verses regarding the issue of the Resurrection and the denial and opposition of the polytheists, the verses under discussion point to the story of one of history's greatest tyrants—Pharaoh—and his painful fate. This serves both to let the Arab polytheists know that individuals far more powerful than them could not withstand Divine wrath and punishment, and to encourage the believers not to harbor fear of the enemy's apparent superior strength, for crushing them is very easy for Allah.

It begins by asking: "Has the story of Moses reached you?" (هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسى‌).

Interestingly, the address is directed to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), and the matter begins with an interrogative to awaken the listener's interest and prepare them to hear this cautionary tale.

Then He adds: "When his Lord called him in the sacred valley of Tuwa" (إِذْ ناداهُ رَبُّهُ بِالْوادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوىً‌). [^1] "Tuwa" may be the name of the sacred land located in the Levant between "Midian" and "Egypt," where the first spark of revelation entered the heart of Moses in that desert. This same expression appears in Surah Ta-Ha, where Moses hears a voice saying: “Indeed, I am your Lord, so remove your sandals. Indeed, you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa” (Ta-Ha: 12). Alternatively, it may have a descriptive meaning derived from the root “tayy” meaning "to fold," as if that land was folded in sanctity and blessing; or as Raghib suggests, Moses had to travel a long path to be ready for revelation, but Allah "folded" this path for him and brought him close to the goal.

Then, He points to the message Allah gave to Moses in that sacred land in two short yet profound sentences, saying: "Go to Pharaoh, for he has transgressed" (اذْهَبْ إِلى‌ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى‌).

"And say to him: 'Would you like to purify yourself?'" (فَقُلْ هَلْ لَكَ إِلى‌ أَنْ تَزَكَّى‌).

"And that I guide you to your Lord so you would fear [Him]?" (وَ أَهْدِيَكَ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ فَتَخْشى‌) — meaning, after becoming pure and worthy of meeting the Beloved, I shall guide you toward your Lord so that you may fear Him and distance yourself from deviant acts.

Since every invitation must be accompanied by proof, the next verse adds: "Then he showed him the great sign" (فَأَراهُ الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرى‌). [^1] Whether this miracle was the staff turning into a massive serpent, or the "White Hand," or both (assuming the definite article in "the Great Sign" refers to the category), whatever it was, it was among the great miracles of Moses upon which he relied at the start of his mission.

In these four verses, there are interesting points that require attention:

  1. He mentions Pharaoh's "transgression" as the reason for going to him, which shows that one of the great goals of the Prophets is to guide tyrants or to decisively struggle against them.

[^1]: Many commentators consider "idh" (when) as a temporal adverb for "hadith" (story). This is correct if "hadith" means the event itself rather than the narration of the event. It is also possible that "idh" is an adverb for a hidden verb, implying "Remember when He called him..." (Reflect on this).

[^1]: It is certain that there was a significant interval between that Divine address in the sacred valley and the showing of this great miracle; the Quran has omitted other details here for the sake of brevity, and they are all implied.

  1. He commands that Pharaoh be invited with the gentlest and most well-wishing expression, saying: "Say to him: Do you have the desire to become pure?" (Absolute purity, which includes purification from polytheism and disbelief as well as from oppression and corruption). This is similar to the expression in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 44: “And speak to him with gentle speech.”

  2. This expression is a subtle allusion to the fact that the mission of the Prophets is to purify human beings and return them to their original primordial purity (fitrah). Furthermore, he does not say "I will purify you," but rather "that you accept purity," indicating that purification must be self-generated and not imposed from without.

  3. Mentioning "guidance" after "purification" is proof that one must first undergo a cleansing before stepping into the presence of the Friend.

  4. The use of the term "your Lord" (rabbuka) is an emphasis on the fact that "I am taking you toward One who is your Owner, Nourisher, and Educator; why do you flee from the path of your own felicity?"

  5. "Fear" (khashyah) and God-fearing are the results of being guided toward Allah. Indeed, the fruit of the tree of guidance and Monotheism is that very sense of responsibility before the Great Lord, for fear never arises without knowledge (ma'rifah). Thus, we read in Surah Fatir, verse 28: “Only those fear Allah, from among His servants, who have knowledge.”

  6. Moses first approaches him through emotional guidance, then through intellectual and logical guidance by showing the "Great Sign" and miracle. This is the most effective way of preaching: first penetrating the emotions of the other party through well-wishing and affection, then presenting arguments and proof.

Now let us see what reaction Pharaoh showed in the face of all this kindness, affection, beautiful logic, and the presentation of the Great Sign. This headstrong tyrant never dismounted from the steed of pride, and as stated in the next verse: "But he denied and disobeyed" (فَكَذَّبَ وَ عَصى‌). This shows that denials are the prelude to acts of disobedience, just as affirmations and faith are the prelude to acts of obedience.

He did not stop at this, nor did he remain indifferent to Moses' invitation; rather, "Then he turned his back, striving [against him]" (ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ يَسْعى‌). [^1]

Since Moses' miracle threatened his entire tyrannical existence, he dispatched officials to various cities to gather sorcerers, and he ordered a proclamation among the people, inviting them to witness the struggle of the sorcerers against Moses: "Then he gathered [his people] and called out" (فَحَشَرَ فَنادى‌). Although the Quran mentions the word "gathered" (hashara) here in general terms, it is clarified by verses 111 and 112 of Al-A'raf: “And send among the cities gatherers who will bring you every learned sorcerer.” The term "called out" (nada), though general, apparently refers to Pharaoh's invitation to the people to assemble and watch the spectacle of this contest, as evidenced by verse 39 of Ash-Shu'ara: “And it was said to the people, 'Will you assemble?'”

[^1]: The use of "thumma" (then), which usually denotes an interval between two events, may be because Pharaoh allowed some time to pass so that his plan to combat Moses could be fully matured and prepared.

He did not stop at these conspiracies either, but put forward the greatest claim with the worst possible expressions and said: "I am your most high lord!!" (فَقالَ أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى‌). Truly, it is strange that when these headstrong tyrants mount the steed of pride and selfishness, they recognize no boundaries; they are not even satisfied with claiming divinity, but want to be the "God of gods"! This statement also implies: "If you worship idols, they are respected in their place, but I am your highest idol and deity!" It is remarkable that, according to verse 127 of Surah Al-A'raf: “Will you leave Moses and his people to cause corruption in the land and abandon you and your gods?” Pharaoh himself was an idol-worshipper, yet here he claims, "I am your most high lord," meaning he considered himself even higher than his own deities—such is the nonsensical talk of tyrants!

Even more remarkable is that in one place he only claims divinity: “I have not known for you any god other than myself” (Al-Qasas: 38), but in the verse under discussion, he goes further and claims Lordship (rububiyyah), saying "I am your Lord." Such are the schemes of these arrogant, light-headed fools.

In any case, Pharaoh reached the final stage of rebellion and deserved the most painful punishment. The Divine command had to arrive to crush him and his apparatus of oppression and corruption. Therefore, the next verse says: "So Allah seized him with an exemplary punishment for the last and the first [transgressions]" (فَأَخَذَهُ اللَّهُ نَكالَ الْآخِرَةِ وَ الْأُولى‌). [^1] "Nakal" (rhyming with dalal) originally means weakness, inability, and helplessness. Thus, of someone who refuses to fulfill a commitment, it is said "nakala" (he recoiled). "Nikl" (rhyming with fikr) means a heavy chain that makes a person weak and unable to move. Since Divine punishment also causes helplessness and deters others from committing sins, it is called nakal.

The expression "punishment of the last" (nakal al-akhirah) refers to the punishments of the Resurrection that overtake Pharaoh and his folk; because it is of primary importance, it is mentioned first. "The first" (al-ula) refers to the punishment of this world that overtook Pharaoh, drowning him and all his companions in the depths of the Nile.

Another interpretation mentioned for the verse is that "the first" refers to the first word Pharaoh spoke in his path of rebellion when he claimed divinity (Al-Qasas: 38), and "the last" refers to the last word he spoke, which was the claim of supreme Lordship. Allah seized him with punishment for these two blasphemous claims in this very world.

[^1]: "Nakal" is in the accusative case due to the removal of a preposition (naz' al-khafid), and it was originally "So Allah seized him with the exemplary punishment..." It is also possible that it is a cognate accusative (maf'ul mutlaq) for the verb "seized," functioning like the verb nakkala (to make an example of).

This meaning is narrated in a tradition from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), and the Imam added that there was a forty-year interval between these two statements (and Allah did not punish him during this time to provide the maximum completion of proof). [^1] This interpretation is more consistent with the word "seized" (akhadha), which is a past-tense verb indicating this punishment fully occurred in the world, and with the following verse which considers this event a lesson.

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, He draws a conclusion from the end of this story, saying: "Indeed in that is a lesson for whoever would fear [Allah]" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِمَنْ يَخْشى‌).

This verse clearly shows that taking a lesson from these events is only possible for those who have allowed a portion of fear, awe, and a sense of responsibility into their hearts, or in other words, possess an eye that can perceive lessons. How fortunate is the eye that sees lessons, Whose custom is to learn from good and evil. Yes, such was the fate of the rebellious Pharaoh, so that other Pharaohs, the leaders of the Arab polytheists, and the followers of Pharaoh's path in every age and time may settle their accounts and know that this is the definitive decree of history and the unchangeable Sunnah of Allah.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 432. Another tradition to the same effect is narrated from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) with more detail and explanation (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 500).

Key Point: A Glimpse of the Quran's Eloquence

Reflecting on the eleven short verses above reveals the ultimate eloquence and rhetoric of the Quran. In these few short lines, a summary of the entire saga of Moses and Pharaoh is reflected: the motive of the mission, the goal of the mission, the means of purification, the manner of invitation, the nature of the reaction and response, the quality of Pharaoh's conspiracy, an example of his vain and baseless claims, and finally, the painful punishments of this individual intoxicated by the wine of pride—and ultimately, the lesson derived from it for all awakened human beings.

Ayahs

ءَأَنتُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقًا أَمِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ ۚ بَنَىٰهَا (٢٧)
Is your creation more prodigious or that of the heaven He has built? (27)
رَفَعَ سَمْكَهَا فَسَوَّىٰهَا (٢٨)
He raised its vault and fashioned it, (28)
وَأَغْطَشَ لَيْلَهَا وَأَخْرَجَ ضُحَىٰهَا (٢٩)
and darkened its night, and brought forth its forenoon. (29)
وَٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ دَحَىٰهَآ (٣٠)
Thereafter He spread out the earth, (30)
أَخْرَجَ مِنْهَا مَآءَهَا وَمَرْعَىٰهَا (٣١)
brought forth from it its water and pastures, (31)
وَٱلْجِبَالَ أَرْسَىٰهَا (٣٢)
setting firm the mountains, (32)
مَتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَـٰمِكُمْ (٣٣)
as a [place of] sustenance for you and your livestock. (33)

Commentary

Is Your Creation More Difficult or the Heavens? (Another Proof of the Resurrection)

Following the narration of the story of Moses and Pharaoh as a lesson for all transgressors and deniers, the discourse returns once again to the issue of the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment. It presents examples of the infinite power of the Truth (Allah) in the universe as a proof for the possibility of the Resurrection, and explains aspects of His endless blessings upon mankind to awaken the sense of gratitude—which is the source of the gnosis of Allah (Ma'rifat Allah)—within them.

First, addressing the deniers of the Resurrection with a reproachful interrogation, He says: "Is your creation (and your return to life after death) more difficult, or the creation of the heaven which Allah has built?" (أَ أَنْتُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقاً أَمِ السَّماءُ بَناها) [^1]. This statement is, in fact, a response to their claim mentioned in the preceding verses where they said: "Shall we indeed be returned to our former state?" (أَ إِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِي الْحافِرَةِ). This verse asserts that any human being, at any level of understanding and intellect, knows that the creation of this lofty heaven with all these massive celestial bodies and infinite galaxies is not comparable to the creation of man. How could He who possesses such power be incapable of returning you to life?

Then, providing further detail regarding this great creation, He adds: "He raised its canopy and ordered it (with harmony and proportion)" (رَفَعَ سَمْكَها فَسَوَّاها).

[^1]: The verse actually has an omitted part, and its estimation is: "or is the heaven more difficult in creation?" and the phrase "He built it" (banaha) is a new sentence (musta'nafah) and a preamble for the subsequent verses.

"Samk" (rhyming with saqf) originally means height and loftiness, and it has also been used to mean "ceiling" or "canopy." In the Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr Razi, it is stated that when the height of something is measured from top to bottom, it is called "depth" ('umq), and when it is measured from bottom to top, it is called "samk" [^1]. "Sawwaha" is derived from the root taswiyah, meaning to regulate and make something balanced; it refers to the precise order that governs all celestial bodies. If "samk" is taken to mean "canopy," it refers to the massive layer of atmosphere that surrounds the Earth like a protected and firm roof, shielding it from the onslaught of scattered celestial stones (meteors) and deadly cosmic rays.

Some consider the above expression to be an allusion to the spherical nature of the atmosphere surrounding the Earth, as taswiyah (leveling/balancing) implies an equal distance of the parts of this canopy from the primary center, i.e., the Earth, and this is not possible without sphericity.

It is also possible that the verse refers both to the height of the heaven and the staggering distances of the celestial bodies from us, as well as the protected canopy around the Earth.

In any case, this verse is similar to what appears in Surah Ghafir (al-Mu'min), verse 57: "The creation of the heavens and the earth is indeed greater than the creation of mankind, but most of mankind know not." (لَخَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 31, p. 46.

Then, He points to one of the most important systems of this great universe, namely the system of light and darkness, saying: "He darkened its night and brought forth its bright morning" (وَ أَغْطَشَ لَيْلَها وَ أَخْرَجَ ضُحاها).

Each of these two plays an extraordinarily important role in the life of humans and other living beings, including animals and plants. Man cannot live without light, as all blessings, sustenance, senses, and movement depend on it; nor is his life possible without darkness, which is the secret of his tranquility [^1]. "Aghtasha" is from the root "ghatash" (rhyming with 'arsh), meaning darkness, but Raghib in al-Mufradat says its origin is from aghtash, referring to someone with weak eyesight.

And "duha" refers to the time when the sunlight spreads across the heaven and the earth [^2].

Then, He moves from the heaven to the earth and says: "And the earth, after that, He spread it out" (وَ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ دَحاها). "Daha" is from the root dahw (rhyming with mahw), meaning to spread out. Some have also interpreted it as moving something from its original place, and since these two meanings are inextricably linked, they return to a single root.

In any case, the meaning of "dahw al-ard" (the spreading of the earth) is that in the beginning, the entire surface of the earth

[^1]: We have had extensive discussions regarding the important effects of light and darkness on the life of living beings in Vol. 8, p. 343; Vol. 12, p. 41; and Vol. 16, p. 146.

[^2]: It is noteworthy that the pronouns in "its night" (laylaha) and "its morning" (duhaha) refer back to the heaven, and the attribution of light and darkness to the heaven is because they have a celestial origin.

was covered by waters resulting from the initial torrential rains. These waters gradually settled into the hollows of the earth, and the dry lands emerged from beneath the water, expanding day by day until they reached their current state (and this event occurred after the creation of the earth and heaven) [^1].

After the expansion of the earth and its preparation for life, He mentions water and plants, saying: "He brought forth therefrom its water and its pasturage" (أَخْرَجَ مِنْها ماءَها وَ مَرْعاها). This expression shows that water was hidden within the permeable layers of the earth, then it flowed out in the form of springs and rivers, and even formed seas and lakes.

"Mar'a" is a noun of place meaning "pasturage" [^2] and is originally derived from "ra'y," meaning to guard and care for an animal, whether through feeding or other aspects. Therefore, mura'at means protection, care, and the management of affairs. The famous Hadith, "Each of you is a shepherd (ra'in) and each of you is responsible for his flock (ra'iyyatih)," also refers to the necessity of people caring for one another [^3].

However, since various factors could disturb the tranquility of the earth—including massive and permanent storms, as well as the tides in the earth's crust caused by the gravity of

[^1]: Some commentators have interpreted the expression "after that" (ba'da dhalika) in the above verse to mean "in addition to this," making the meaning: "In addition to what was mentioned in the previous verses, He spread the earth."

[^2]: Some have also taken it as a mim-infinitive (masdar mimi) meaning "grazing," but the aforementioned meaning is more appropriate.

[^3]: Mufradat al-Raghib, root "ra'y."

the moon and the sun, as well as tremors caused by the pressure of internal molten materials—He stabilized it by means of a powerful network of mountains that cover the entire earth. Thus, He says: "And the mountains, He firmly fixed them" (وَ الْجِبالَ أَرْساها) [^1]. We have had a detailed discussion regarding the important role of mountains in human life and the tranquility of the earth under verse 3 of Surah al-Ra'd (Vol. 10, p. 114).

And in conclusion, He says that He did all this to be a means of "provision for you and for your cattle" (مَتاعاً لَكُمْ وَ لِأَنْعامِكُمْ). Yes, He raised the heaven.

And established the system of light and darkness.

He spread out the earth.

And brought forth water and plants from it.

He made the mountains the guardians of the earth's stability.

And provided all the means for human life.

And He made all of them subservient and obedient for his sake.

So that man may benefit from the blessings of life and not fall into heedlessness, and in gratitude for all these obedient servants created for him, he should obey the Divine command.

These are, on one hand, signs of His power over the issue of the Resurrection, and on the other hand, proofs of His greatness and signs of His existence on the path of Monotheism (Tawhid) and Gnosis.

[^1]: "Arsa" is from the root "rasw" (rhyming with rasm), meaning to be firm or fixed, and "arsa" is its transitive form, meaning He made the mountains firm and stable.

Ayahs

فَإِذَا جَآءَتِ ٱلطَّآمَّةُ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰ (٣٤)
When the Greatest Catastrophe befalls (34)
يَوْمَ يَتَذَكَّرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَا سَعَىٰ (٣٥)
—the day when man will remember his endeavours (35)
وَبُرِّزَتِ ٱلْجَحِيمُ لِمَن يَرَىٰ (٣٦)
and hell is brought into view for those who can see— (36)
فَأَمَّا مَن طَغَىٰ (٣٧)
as for him who has been rebellious (37)
وَءَاثَرَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا (٣٨)
and preferred the life of this world, (38)
فَإِنَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ هِىَ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰ (٣٩)
his refuge will indeed be hell. (39)
وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَافَ مَقَامَ رَبِّهِۦ وَنَهَى ٱلنَّفْسَ عَنِ ٱلْهَوَىٰ (٤٠)
But as for him who is awed to stand before his Lord and restrains his soul from [following] desires, (40)
فَإِنَّ ٱلْجَنَّةَ هِىَ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰ (٤١)
his refuge will indeed be paradise. (41)

Commentary

Those Who Restrain the Soul from Whims and Desires

After the reference to some of the proofs of the Resurrection in the preceding verses## Commentary

Those Who Restrain the Soul from Whims and Desires

After the reference to some of the proofs of the Resurrection in the preceding verses, the verses under discussion return once more to the issue of the Resurrection and the fate of the God-fearing and the ego-worshippers on that Day, stating: "When that great event occurs, the righteous and the wicked shall each receive the recompense for their deeds" (فَإِذا جاءَتِ الطَّامَّةُ الْكُبْرى‌) [1]. The word Tammah (طامة) is derived from the root Tamm (on the weight of Fann), which originally means "to fill." Anything that reaches the highest degree is called Tammah; hence, severe events and great calamities filled with difficulties are also referred to as Tammah. Here, it refers to the Day of Judgment, which is filled with terrifying events, and describing it as Kubra (the Greatest) is a further emphasis on the importance and magnitude of this unparalleled event.

Then it adds that when this great event occurs, everyone will awaken from the sleep of negligence, and man will remember his efforts, strivings, and deeds, whether good or bad: (يَوْمَ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ ما سَعى‌). But what benefit can this remembrance and recollection have for him? If he requests to return to the world to compensate for the past, he will be rejected, and in response to this request, they will say "Nay" (Kalla).

[1]: Idha (اذا) is a conditional particle, and according to a group of commentators, its response (Jaza) is found in the subsequent verses (فَأَمَّا مَنْ طَغى‌ ... وَ أَمَّا مَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ‌ ...). However, it is better to say that its response is omitted and can be inferred from the following verses, as if it were: "When the Greatest Calamity comes, every human shall be recompensed for what he did." Some have also suggested that the response is inferred from (يَوْمَ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ), but this possibility is remote.

And if he repents and seeks an excuse for his deeds, it will be of no use, for the doors of repentance have already been closed.

Therefore, he has no choice but to heave sighs of regret and sorrow, and as the Quran says, to bite both his hands with his teeth: (يَوْمَ يَعضُّ الظَّالِمُ عَلى يَدَيْهِ) "The day when the wrongdoer will bite his hands" (Al-Furqan: 27).

It should be noted that Yatadhakkaru (يتذكر) is a present-tense verb, which usually denotes continuity; meaning on that Day, man will continuously remember all his deeds. This is because on that Day, the veils will be lifted from the heart and soul of man, and all hidden realities will become manifest and clear.

Therefore, in the next verse, it adds: "On that Day, Hell shall be made manifest for every beholder" (وَ بُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِمَنْ يَرى‌). Hell exists even now; rather, according to verse 54 of Surah Al-Ankabut: (وَ إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ) "And indeed, Hell encompasses the disbelievers," it surrounds the disbelievers from every side, but the veils of the worldly realm prevent its vision. However, on that Day, which is the day of the manifestation and emergence of everything, Hell will appear most clearly.

The phrase Li-man yara (for him who sees) indicates that Hell on that Day will be so manifest that every beholder, without exception, will see it, and it will be hidden from no one—neither the righteous nor the wicked whose abode is Hell.

The possibility has also been raised that the phrase refers to those who possess sight on that Day, because according to verse 124 of Surah Taha, some will be gathered blind on that Day: (وَ نَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَعْمى‌). However, the first meaning, which most commentators have accepted, seems more appropriate, as the sight of Hell is itself a double punishment and chastisement for the wicked. Furthermore, the blindness of a group in the gathering place might occur at certain stations and not all of them [1].

Then, it refers to the state of the criminals and faithless individuals on the scene of the Resurrection, and with a few short and meaningful sentences, it describes both their fate and the factors of their predicament, saying: "As for him who rebelled" (فَأَمَّا مَنْ طَغى‌).

"And preferred the life of this world over everything else" (وَ آثَرَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا).

"Surely Hell shall be his abode and refuge" (فَإِنَّ الْجَحِيمَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى‌) [2]. In the first sentence, it refers to their ideological corruption, for rebellion (Tughyan) stems from self-aggrandizement, and self-aggrandizement stems from a lack of Ma'rifah (knowledge) of Allah. One who recognizes Allah in His greatness sees himself as very small and weak and never steps outside the path of servitude.

The second sentence refers to their practical corruption, for rebellion causes man to consider the fleeting pleasures of the world and its glitter as the highest value and to prefer them over everything else.

These two are, in fact, cause and effect of one another: rebellion and ideological corruption are the source of practical corruption and the preference for the unstable life of the world over everything else, and the end result of these two is the blazing fire of Hell.

Imam Ali عليه السلام says in a Hadith: "Whoever rebels goes astray in action without any proof" [1]. This stems from that same self-aggrandizement where man accepts his desires without any logical reason and grants them value.

Then, it proceeds to mention the attributes of the people of Paradise in two short and very meaningful sentences, saying: "But as for him who feared the standing before his Lord and restrained his soul from whims and desires..." (وَ أَمَّا مَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ وَ نَهَى النَّفْسَ عَنِ الْهَوى‌).

"Paradise shall be his abode and refuge" (فَإِنَّ الْجَنَّةَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى‌). Yes, the first condition for becoming a person of Paradise is "fear" stemming from "knowledge"—recognizing the station of the Lord and fearing the violation of His command. The second condition, which is in fact the result of the first condition and the fruit of the tree of knowledge and fear, is mastery over the whims of the soul and restraining it from rebellion. For all sins, corruptions, and miseries stem from the whims of the soul; the whim of the soul is the worst idol that has been worshipped: "The most hated god worshipped on the face of the earth is the whim (Hawa)."

Even the tool for Satan's influence in the existence of man is the "whim of the soul"; if this "internal Satan" does not harmonize with the "external Satan" and does not open the door to him, his entry is impossible, just as the Quran says:

[1]: Further explanation in this regard is provided in Volume 13, page 330, under verse 124 of Taha.

[2]: The verse has an omitted part, and it is implied as Hiya al-ma'wa lahu (it is the abode for him) or Hiya ma'wahu (it is his abode), and the pronoun was omitted for clarity.

(إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ‌) "Indeed, My servants—you have no authority over them, except those who follow you of the deviators" (Al-Hijr: 42).

Key Points

1- What is the "Station of the Lord" (Maqam-i Rabb)?

It is noteworthy that in the verses under discussion, it says: "He who feared the station of his Lord," and it does not say: "He who feared his Lord." What is meant by "station" (Maqam)?

There are several interpretations here:

  1. First, it refers to the "stations of the Resurrection" where man stops at these stations in the presence of Allah for accounting. (According to this interpretation, Maqama Rabbihi means Maqamahu 'inda Rabbihi, i.e., man's standing before his Lord).

  2. It refers to "Allah's knowledge and His station of oversight" over all humans, as mentioned in verse 33 of Ar-Ra'd: (أَ فَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى‌ كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ‌) "Is He then who is standing over every soul [observing] what it has earned [like any other]?" Another witness for this interpretation is a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام who said: "Whoever knows that Allah sees him, hears what he says, and knows what he does of good or evil, and this prevents him from ugly deeds, he is the one who feared the station of his Lord and restrained his soul from whims" [1].

  3. It refers to His "station of justice," for His Holy Essence is not a source of fear; the fear is of His justice. In reality, this fear arises from comparing one's deeds with His justice, just as criminals tremble at the sight of a just judge and are terrified by the name of the court, while an innocent person has neither fear of the former nor terror of the latter! There is no contradiction among these three interpretations, and it is possible that all of them are gathered in the meaning of the verse.

2- The Relationship Between "Rebellion" and "World-Worship"

In truth, the concise verses above have beautifully and appropriately depicted the principles of human happiness and wretchedness. They consider human wretchedness to lie in "rebellion" and "world-worship," and his happiness in "fear of Allah" and "abandoning whims." This is the essence of all the teachings of the Prophets and the Friends of Allah, and nothing else.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "The most terrifying things I fear for you are two: following whims and long-held hopes. As for following whims, it bars one from the truth; as for long-held hopes, they make one forget the Hereafter" [2].

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 197.

[2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 42.

Ego-worship draws a veil over man's intellect, beautifies his evil deeds in his eyes, and takes away from him the sense of discernment—which is the greatest blessing of God and the distinction between man and animal—and keeps him preoccupied with himself. This is what Jacob, that enlightened Prophet, said to his misbehaving sons: (بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنْفُسُكُمْ أَمْراً) "Rather, your souls have enticed you to something" (Yusuf: 18).

There is much to say here, but it is better to conclude this discussion by referring to two Hadiths from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام that say all that needs to be said:

Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام says: "Paradise is surrounded by hardships and patience; so whoever is patient with hardships in the world enters Paradise. And Hell is surrounded by pleasures and lusts; so whoever gives his soul its pleasure and lust enters the Fire" [1].

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "Do not leave the soul to its whims, for its whim lies in its destruction; leaving the soul to what it desires is its disease, and restraining the soul from what it desires is its medicine" [2].

Not only is the Hell of the Hereafter born of ego-worship, but the blazing hells of this world—such as insecurity, disorder, wars, bloodshed, disputes, and grudges—all stem from it.

3- Only Two Groups

In the above verses, only two groups are mentioned: the world-worshipping rebels, and the God-fearing pious ones. The permanent abode of the first group is mentioned as Hell, and the eternal abode of the second group as Paradise.

Of course, there is also a third group: believers who have been negligent or tainted in terms of action. If they are included in Divine forgiveness and are worthy of it, they will join the people of Paradise; if they are not included, they will go to Hell, but it will not be their Ma'wa (permanent abode). However, there is no mention of them in the above verses.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 507, Hadith 46.

[2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 507, Hadith 45.

Ayahs

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْسَىٰهَا (٤٢)
They ask you concerning the Hour, “When will it set in, (42)
فِيمَ أَنتَ مِن ذِكْرَىٰهَآ (٤٣)
considering your frequent mention of it?” (43)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ مُنتَهَىٰهَآ (٤٤)
Its outcome is with your Lord. (44)
إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُنذِرُ مَن يَخْشَىٰهَا (٤٥)
You are only a warner for those who are afraid it. (45)
كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَهَا لَمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا عَشِيَّةً أَوْ ضُحَىٰهَا (٤٦)
The day they see it, it shall be as if they had not stayed [in the world] except for an evening or forenoon. (46)

Commentary

Only Allah Knows the Timing of the Resurrection

Following the discussions regarding the Resurrection and the fate of the righteous and the wicked in the preceding verses, these verses address the perennial question of the polytheists and deniers of the Hereafter, stating:

"They ask you about the Hour: when will it take place?" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها) [^1]

In response to this question, and to make them understand that no one has been or will ever be informed of the moment the Resurrection occurs, the Quran addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "What have you to do with mentioning it?" (فِيمَ أَنْتَ مِنْ ذِكْراها). This means that the date of the Resurrection's occurrence is hidden even from you, let alone others. This is part of the knowledge of the Unseen (Ghayb) which is exclusive to the Essence of the Lord; no one has access to it!

We have stated many times that the timing of the Resurrection is among the matters concealed from everyone, because its educational effect is impossible without such concealment. If it were revealed and the time were far off, negligence would overtake everyone; and if it were near, abstaining from evils would take the form of desperation, devoid of freedom and choice, and both scenarios would lack educational value.

Other possibilities have also been suggested in the interpretation of this verse, including: You were not sent to explain the timing of the Resurrection; you were only sent to inform of its fundamental existence, not the moment of its occurrence.

[^1]: "Mursa" (مرسا) is used as an infinitive (Masdar), a noun of time and place, and a passive participle. Derived from the root "Irsa" (ارسا), here it carries the infinitive meaning, signifying occurrence, stability, and becoming established. This expression is used regarding a ship docking, as well as the state of stability of mountains on the earth, such as: "And [Noah] said, 'Embark therein; in the name of Allah is its course and its anchorage (mursaha)'" (Hud: 41) and "And the mountains He set firmly (arsaha)" (An-Nazi'at: 32).

Another interpretation is: Your rising [as a Prophet] is an indicator of the approach of the Day of Resurrection, as narrated in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله where he held two of his fingers together and said:

"I and the Hour have been sent like these two!" [^1] However, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all.

Then it adds: "To your Lord is its finality" (إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ مُنْتَهاها). He alone knows when someone's Resurrection will be established; no other person is aware of it, and any effort or struggle to gain knowledge of the time of the Resurrection is futile.

This is the same point mentioned in verse 34 of Surah Luqman: "Indeed, Allah [alone] has knowledge of the Hour" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ), and in verse 187 of Surah Al-A'raf, He says: "Say, 'Its knowledge is only with my Lord'" (قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي).

Some have said the meaning of the above sentence is that the realization and execution of the Resurrection are in the hand of Allah, and in reality, it serves as an explanation of the cause for what appeared in the previous verse.

There is no obstacle to combining these two interpretations.

[^1]: Tafsir "Fakhr Razi," Vol. 29, p. 29. This point is also mentioned in Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," "Qurtubi," "Fi Zilal," and other commentaries under verse 18 of Surah Muhammad.

To provide further clarification, it adds: "You are only a warner for those who fear it" (إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرُ مَنْ يَخْشاها).

Your duty is only this warning, alerting, and frightening, and nothing else; as for determining the time, it is outside the domain of your duty and knowledge.

It is noteworthy that He restricts the warning to those who have fear and dread of that Great Day. This is similar to what is mentioned in the second verse of Surah Al-Baqarah: "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those conscious of Allah (the Muttaqin)" (ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ). Such expressions point to the reality that unless the spirit of truth-seeking and a sense of responsibility before the Lord exists within a person, they will neither seek to investigate the Heavenly Books nor the Hereafter, nor will they listen to the warnings of the Prophets and the Awliya (Saints).

Finally, in the last verse of this Surah, to express the reality that there is not much time until the Resurrection, He says: "The day they see it, it will be as if they had not remained [in the world] except for an afternoon or a morning thereof" (كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَها لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا عَشِيَّةً أَوْ ضُحاها). The short life of this world passes so quickly, and the period of Barzakh (the intermediate realm) is traversed so rapidly, that at the time of the Resurrection, they will think this entire period was no more than a few hours. This matter is inherently true—that the life of the world is very short and fleeting—and also true in comparison to the Resurrection, for the entire life of the world is no more than a moment compared to it.

"Ashiyyah" (عشية) means evening or afternoon, and "Duha" (ضحى) refers to the time when the sun has risen and its rays have spread.

In some verses of the Quran, it is mentioned that on the Day of Resurrection, when the criminals discuss the length of their stay in the realm of Barzakh or the world, some say to others: You stayed in the world of Barzakh for only ten days:

"They will whisper among themselves, 'You remained not but ten [days]'" (Taha: 103).

But those who think more clearly will say: You stayed in Barzakh for only a day! "The most perceptive of them in course will say, 'You remained not but a day.'"

And elsewhere, it narrates from the criminals that when the Resurrection is established, the criminals will swear that they stayed for only an hour! "And the Day the Hour appears the criminals will swear they had remained but an hour" (Ar-Rum: 55).

The difference in these expressions is because they want to reflect the shortness of this duration with an approximate statement, and each expresses their feeling with a term, all of which share one common point: the shortness of the life of this world compared to the life of the Resurrection. This is a matter that shakes a person and awakens them from the sleep of negligence.

O Allah! Grant us all security and peace on that Great Day, and in this world and the world of Barzakh. O Lord! No one finds salvation from the hardships of that Great Day except by Your grace; make us recipients of Your special grace.

O God! Place us among those who fear Your station, restrain their souls from low desires, and take refuge in the Exalted Paradise. [[^1]] Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah "An-Nazi'at."

[^1]: These lines were written during hours when there was a constant possibility of aerial bombardment by the infidel Ba'athist regime of Iraq and being buried under rubble. One or two days before this date, in one of these bombardments in this holy city of Qom, about one hundred and fifty Muslims, especially innocent children, were martyred in a brutal bombing. But it is certain that the life of oppression and the oppressor will be short.
